title: scenario_oauth_phishing
description: An attacker tricks employees into granting a malicious third-party application access to corporate Microsoft 365 data via a fake OAuth consent screen.

timeline.title: Fake App Consent Email
timeline.description: Employees receive emails with links to a Microsoft-branded consent page asking them to authorise a 'Productivity Booster' app.

timeline.title: Multiple Employees Consent
timeline.description: Several employees click through and grant the app access to their email and files without reading the permissions requested.

timeline.title: Data Exfiltration Begins
timeline.description: The malicious app begins silently reading and forwarding emails and files from consenting accounts.

timeline.title: Anomalous App Access Flagged
timeline.description: Microsoft Entra ID reports an unrecognised third-party application with broad permissions across multiple accounts.

timeline.title: App Permissions Revoked
timeline.description: IT revokes the malicious app's OAuth tokens across all affected accounts.

timeline.title: App Allowlist Policy Enforced
timeline.description: Tenant settings updated to block all third-party OAuth app consents without admin pre-approval.

node-1.title: Suspicious App Consent Request
node-1.situation: Employees have been emailed a link asking them to click 'Authorise' on what looks like a genuine Microsoft consent screen. The app called 'ProSync 365' requests permissions to read and send emails and access all files on OneDrive on the user's behalf.
node-1.question: What should employees do when they receive this consent request?

options.label: A
options.text: Do not authorise the app. Report the email to IT security and check whether the app is on the approved software list.

options.label: B
options.text: Authorise the app — the Microsoft branding on the consent screen means it has been vetted by Microsoft.

options.label: C
options.text: Authorise only read access to email but not files — partial consent is safe.

node-2.title: Several Employees Already Consented
node-2.situation: IT discovers six employees already authorised the app before the warning went out. The app now has OAuth tokens granting it ongoing access to their accounts, even without their passwords.
node-2.question: How do you revoke the malicious access?

options.label: A
options.text: Revoke all OAuth tokens for the malicious app across all affected accounts via the Microsoft Entra ID admin console immediately.

options.label: B
options.text: Ask the affected employees to change their passwords — this will remove the app's access.

node-3.title: Data Exfiltration Assessment
node-3.situation: Audit logs show the app read approximately 400 emails and accessed 15 files in the 2-hour window it was active. Some emails contained client details and internal financial figures.
node-3.question: What is required following the data exposure?

options.label: A
options.text: Conduct a full data impact assessment, notify affected clients if their data was exposed, and report the breach to the relevant data protection authority.

options.label: B
options.text: The access window was short — no notification is necessary.

node-4.title: Preventing Future OAuth Consent Phishing
node-4.situation: IT proposes restricting all third-party OAuth app consents so only admin-approved apps can be authorised by users.
node-4.question: Should this restriction be applied across the entire Microsoft 365 tenant?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes — configure Entra ID to require admin consent for all third-party apps, and build a lightweight app approval process for legitimate business needs.

options.label: B
options.text: No — blocking all third-party apps will be too disruptive to productivity.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: The malicious app's tokens have been revoked, affected parties have been notified, and the Microsoft 365 tenant is now configured to require admin consent for any third-party OAuth application. Staff have been trained to recognise OAuth consent phishing.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
